Psalm 120 opens the final collection of psalms with a cry that many of us know too well: the desperate plea to be rescued from lies. The psalmist's distress is not primarily physical danger but relational betrayal—he lives among people whose words are weapons. Notice that he calls out to the Lord first, and the Lord answers. This is not a distant God who ignores His people's anguish. But the answer is not what we might expect: God does not immediately remove him from his enemies. Instead, the psalm moves into a prophecy of divine justice—arrows and burning coals will come upon the deceitful tongue. The psalmist is not indulging in revenge fantasy; he is declaring that God Himself will reckon with those who weaponize language. There is a terrible weight here: the tongue is so significant to God that He promises to judge it severely. If you have ever been destroyed by words, this should grip you. Someone's lies about you matter to God enough that He has promised judgment.

What makes this psalm particularly piercing is the psalmist's helplessness. He does not ask for strength to fight back or wisdom to out-debate his enemies. He asks to be delivered from them. And then he does something almost despairing—he admits that he has dwelt too long in this place. Meshech and Kedar were distant, foreign lands in the ancient world, symbolic of being far from home. But the psalmist is describing something deeper: he feels like an exile among hostile people. He seeks peace, but when he speaks for it, they demand war. This is the unbearable tension of being a person of principle among those committed to conflict. He cannot escape it. He cannot make them understand. His very plea for peace becomes ammunition against him.

Yet here is what we must not miss: the psalmist brings this entire burden—his distress, his frustration, his sense of displacement, his helplessness—directly to the Lord. He does not resolve it himself. He does not pretend the situation is not as bad as it is. He names it precisely: deceitful lips, a long dwelling among enemies, the perverse reversal where his peace-seeking is treated as hostility. And in presenting all of this to God, he trusts that the Lord has seen it, understands it, and will act. The psalm does not end with the psalmist feeling better about his circumstances. It ends with him having appealed to a God who judges the tongue and vindicates His people. If you are living among those whose words betray you, or if you have spoken truth only to find it twisted, this psalm invites you to stop trying to fix it yourself. Bring your case to the Lord. He hears. He answers. And He will have the final word.
